content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 607 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

March 16, 1977

A-77-347

Edward W. Bent, Chairman
California Fire Services Coordinating Council
11th and L Building, Suite 234
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Bent:

Thank you for your letter of February 22, 1977, re-
questing advice with respect to whether the California Fire
Services Coordinating Council has incurred any obligations
pursuant to the Political Reform Act of 1974. After studying
your letter, it is the conclusion of our staff that the Cali-
fornia Fire Services Coordinating Council, as presently constitu-
ted, is not subject to any of the provisions of the Political
Reform Act.

As I understand the facts, the California Fire Ser-
vices Coordinating Council (CFSCC) has no compensated employees
and does not reimburse any member for time or expenses in con-
nection with attempting to influence legislation. Virtually
all of the expenses of the CFSCC relate to costs incurred in
connection with typing and mailing meeting notices, minutes
and letters. The only portion of these expenses which are
directed at influencing legislative action are those involving
letters setting forth CFSCC positions on specific legislation.
It is my understanding that these costs fall short of the $250
per month threshold set forth in Government Code Section 86108(b).
The CFSCC also does not make gifts or contributions to any legis-
lative or administrative officials or receive contributions
itself.

Under these facts, the CFSCC is not subject to any
of the provisions of the Political Reform Act. If you have any
further questions, please feel free to contact either Ted Prim
or me.

Sincerely,

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

MB:TP:jo

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE AVAILABLE - CALL (916) 445-7635

CALIFORNIA
FIRE SERVICES
COORDINATING COUNCIL

COUNCIL MEMBERS

California Division of Forestry

California Division of Forestry
Fire Prevention Association

California Fire Chiefs' Association

California Fire Service
Training Program

California Rural Fire Association

California State Employed
Fire Fighters Association

California State
Firemen's Association

Federated Fire Fighters of California

Fire and Rescue Division
Office of Emergency Services

League-California Cities
Fire Chief's Section

Office of the State Fire Marshal

Special Districts
Fire Association of California

California Teamsters
Public Affairs Council

February 22, 1977

Commission on Fair Political Practices
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Gentlemen:

The California Fire Services Coordinating Council was
formed in 1973 to act as a forum where all segments
of California's Fire Service could openly and frankly
discuss current problems of mutual interest. Thus
membership is made up of representatives from State
Agencies involved in fire protection such as the California
Department of Forestry and the Office of the State
Fire Marshal; fire service labor groups such as the
California State Firemen's Association and the Federated
Fire Fighters; and finally, fire service management
organizations such as the Fire Chiefs' Department of
the League of California Cities and the California
Fire Chiefs' Association.

At our last meeting, one of our members asked if any
of the provisions of the Fair Political Practices Act
were applicable to an organization operating in the manner
of the Coordinating Council. No one knew the answer to
this question so the membership instructed me to describe
our operation in full to the FPPC and seek an official
answer.

Our method of operations is as follows. The Council meets
on a somewhat irregular schedule, but generally once each
month. Any topic of mutual interest may be brought up
for discussion including current legislation. With respect
to the latter, there are occasional instances where there
is a consensus opinion among the factions represented on
the Council, i.e. State agencies, Fire Services Labor and
Fire Service Management, and that fact can then be used
by any of the individual member organizations in their
independent efforts to influence the applicable legislation.
When such a consensus does exist, it is not unusual for
the Council to transmit this information via a letter to
an appropriate legislator.

11th & L BUILDING • SUITE 234 • SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814

FPPC                           -2-                    February 22, 1977


The Council has no paid staff, no regular dues, no formal organi-
zation. At each meeting, one member acts as a chairman for the
discussion and another member voluntarily acts as a secretary
so that minutes may be prepared and distributed to the members.

Funds for stationery, postage and so forth are obtained by periodi-
cally assessing various member organizations. The Council has
never expended funds entertaining any legislator and has never
donated money, name or services to any legislator's election
campaign. What funds we do have are kept for us by the
California State Firemen's Association and those are used exclu-
sively for the various and miscellaneous administrative needs of
the Council itself such as mailing out meeting notices to our
members. A financial statement, covering the entire four years
of our existence, is attached.

The question is, are we in violation of any FPPC rules and if so,
what corrective actions must we take?

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        EDWARD W. BENT
                                        Chairman
